edits.slides.0.description: No brief, no client, no user research — so I gave myself one constraint: make Wikipedia's techno content feel like the music sounds. Built solo in Figma and Webflow.
edits.slides.1.content: Wikipedia covers the history. None of it feels like the music. My rule: take the same source and make it feel like what it describes.
edits.slides.1.issues.0: Dense text walls, no visual hierarchy — information, not experience
edits.slides.1.issues.1: Generic sidebar makes every topic feel identical
edits.slides.2.dir1Desc: Text-forward redesign — clean type over Wikipedia's layout. It read like a design exercise, not a cultural experience.
edits.slides.2.dir2Desc: Visual maximalism — photography, gradients, full-bleed texture. The decoration competed with the story instead of carrying it.
edits.slides.2.dir3Desc: Rhythmic restraint — monochrome grain, ASCII portraits, scroll-triggered reveals. The look captures the underground feel without spelling it out. Works across every screen size.
edits.slides.3.description: Monochrome grain, ASCII silhouettes, oversized type — every visual choice earned its place. Nothing made it in unless the content demanded it.
edits.slides.4.whatYouLearned: Controlling where attention lands — through scroll timing or through a multi-step workflow — is the same design problem. That insight transfers.
edits.slides.4.whatYoudDoDifferently: Test three scroll speeds with five readers before going live — pacing is the hardest thing to judge alone.
